perturbation.original_text: 4.2 TERMINATION FOR BREACH. In addition to any other rights or remedies that may be available at law or in equity, either party may terminate this Agreement if the other party is in material breach of this Agreement and has not cured the breach within thirty (30) days of receiving written notice specifying the breach. If the breach is not cured within the thirty (30) day period, termination will become effective on the thirty-first (31st) day following the written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 TERMINATION FOR BREACH. In addition to any other rights or remedies that may be available at law or in equity, either party may terminate this Agreement if the other party is in material breach of this Agreement and has not cured the breach within ten (10) days of receiving written notice specifying the breach. If the breach is not cured within the ten (10) day period, termination will become effective on the eleventh (11th) day following the written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to 10 days may conflict with state laws that require a longer cure period for certain types of contracts or breaches, particularly those involving significant investments or ongoing obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1161 requires a 3-day notice to cure for unlawful detainer, but a commercial lease may require a longer cure period. Setting the contractual cure period to 10 days when certain breaches might legally require longer (depending on the lease terms and situation) creates a contradiction, potentially invalidating the termination clause.

(Amended (as added by Stats. 2020, Ch. 37, Sec. 16) by Stats. 2024, Ch. 287, Sec. 6.)
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a 30-day cure period for a material breach, while the changed text reduces it to 10 days. The Law Explanation points to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1161, which requires a 3-day notice to cure for unlawful detainer, but notes that commercial leases may require a longer cure period. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that reducing the cure period to 10 days may conflict with state laws, especially those involving significant investments or ongoing obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 acknowledges multiple results when searching Code of Civil Procedure - CCP with '1161.'. While Snippet 2 is empty, the potential contradiction lies in the fact that depending on the specific contract and applicable law, 10 days might be insufficient, creating a legal risk and undermining established legal standards for cure periods. This constitutes a contradiction because the modified text potentially violates federal or state law, as per the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 12.12 NOTICES. Notices required hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed given when transmitted by facsimile (provided such facsimile is subsequently confirmed in writing within five (5) days of the facsimile date) or deposited with an express delivery service with guaranteed third-day delivery, prepaid, addressed as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: 12.12 NOTICES. Notices required hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed given when transmitted by standard mail, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only standard mail for contractual notices might be insufficient under certain state laws that require specific methods of delivery (e.g., certified mail, return receipt requested) for legal notices to ensure proof of service, especially for important matters like contract termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 extends deadlines when service is by mail. Removing the options for facsimile or guaranteed delivery and only allowing standard mail introduces delays, potentially affecting critical timelines (like cure periods or termination notices) and contradicting legal presumptions about effective service dates. Also, it removes ways to confirm that a notice was sent and received.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The change from allowing facsimile and guaranteed third-day delivery to only allowing standard mail contradicts legal principles related to timely and verifiable notice. California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013(a) acknowledges that service by mail extends the response time by five to ten days, recognizing the inherent delays in mail delivery. By removing faster, more reliable options like guaranteed delivery, the contract introduces potential delays and uncertainties regarding when notice is deemed given, as explained by the Law Explanation. This contradicts the need for timely and verifiable notice, which is especially important in contractual settings where deadlines matter.

perturbation.original_text: 8.3 Each party agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the disclosure of Confidential Information, it (a) will not use, directly or indirectly, or reproduce the Confidential Information of the other party for any purpose except in accordance with the terms of the Agreement, (b) will not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party to any third parties except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, and (c) will take all reasonable security measures to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information of the other party in its possession or control, which will in no event be less than the measures it uses to maintain the confidentiality of its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.3 Each party agrees that for a period of one (1) year following the disclosure of Confidential Information, it (a) will not use, directly or indirectly, or reproduce the Confidential Information of the other party for any purpose except in accordance with the terms of the Agreement, (b) will not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party to any third parties except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, and (c) will take all reasonable security measures to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information of the other party in its possession or control, which will in no event be less than the measures it uses to maintain the confidentiality of its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic if the confidential information involves trade secrets. State trade secret laws offer protection as long as the information remains a trade secret. A one-year contractual term might be insufficient to protect trade secrets with a longer useful life, potentially undermining the protection afforded by state law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 3426-3426.11)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality agreement to one year undermines this protection by implying that after one year, the information is no longer confidential, even if it still qualifies as a trade secret under the law. This creates a conflict where the contract offers less protection than state law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation states that this Act protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting the confidentiality agreement to one year, the modified contract implies that the information is no longer confidential after that period, even if it still qualifies as a trade secret, creating a conflict where the contract offers less protection than state law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Civil Code Section 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating the applicability of this law. The Perturbation Explanation also points out that a one-year contractual term might be insufficient to protect trade secrets with a longer useful life, potentially undermining state law.
